  1264	Choose from the following options: [A] the first photographic exhibition
  1265	in history [B] the first use of color photography [C] a court decision that
  1266	determined that photographs could be considered works of art [D] the invention
  1267	of the zoopraxiscope
  1268	Explanation for Problem 1: The painting depicts Nadar, a famous French
  1269	photographer, raising a camera to the sky, as if to signify the recognition
  1270	of photography as a legitimate form of art. This recognition would have
  1271	likely come from a court decision, rather than the other options listed. The
  1272	first photographic exhibition and the invention of the zoopraxiscope, which
  1273	was a device used to create moving images, would not have had any bearing
  1274	on the recognition of photography as art. Similarly, the first use of color
  1275	photography would not have been a significant enough event to warrant such a
  1276	celebratory depiction in the painting. Therefore, the most likely explanation
  1277	for the painting is that it was done in response to a court decision that
  1278	determined that photographs could be considered works of art.
  1279	The answer is therefore [C]

  1283	Mannerism [D] Baroque

  1287	ornate and elaborate styles, which were heavily influenced by the Catholic
  1288	Church and its religious themes. This style was well-suited to the art
  1289	produced in New Spain, which was heavily influenced by the Catholic Church
  1290	and its religious themes. In contrast, the other options listed were not
  1291	as influential in New Spain during this time period. The Renaissance and
  1292	Mannerism were primarily focused on classical themes and styles, which were
  1293	not as prevalent in New Spain. Similarly, Romanticism was a later movement

  1828	GPT-4 System Card
  1829	OpenAI
  1830	Abstract
  1831	Large language models (LLMs) are being deployed in many domains of our lives ranging
  1832	from browsing, to voice assistants, to coding assistance tools, and have potential for vast societal
  1833	impacts.[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] This system card analyzes GPT-4, the latest LLM in the GPT family
  1834	of models.[8, 9, 10] First, we highlight safety challenges presented by the model’s limitations
  1835	(e.g., producing convincing text that is subtly false) and capabilities (e.g., increased adeptness
  1836	at providing illicit advice, performance in dual-use capabilities, and risky emergent behaviors).
  1837	Second, we give a high-level overview of the safety processes OpenAI adopted to prepare GPT-4
  1838	for deployment. This spans our work across measurements, model-level changes, product- and
  1839	system-level interventions (such as monitoring and policies), and external expert engagement.
  1840	Finally, we demonstrate that while our mitigations and processes alter GPT-4’s behavior and
  1841	prevent certain kinds of misuses, they are limited and remain brittle in some cases. This points
  1842	to the need for anticipatory planning and governance.[11]
  1843	Content Warning: This document contains content that some may find disturbing or offensive,
  1844	including content that is sexual, hateful, or violent in nature.

  1848	1 Introduction
  1849	Large language models, also known as LLMs, have become an increasingly prevalent part of our
  1850	day-to-day lives, with their use extending to a wide range of domains including web browsing, voice
  1851	assistants, and coding assistance tools.[1, 2, 3, 4] These models have the potential to significantly
  1852	impact society in numerous ways.[5, 6, 7] This system card analyzes GPT-4, the latest large language
  1853	model in the GPT family of models.[8, 9, 10] Since it finished training in August of 2022, we have
  1854	been evaluating, adversarially testing, and iteratively improving the model and the system-level
  1855	mitigations around it. Our mitigations and processes alter GPT-4’s behavior and prevent certain
  1856	kinds of misuses, though they have limitations, pointing to the need for anticipatory planning and
  1857	governance[11] and further safety research. Our approach to deployment balances minimizing risk
  1858	from deployment, enabling positive use cases, and learning from deployment.
  1859	GPT models are often trained in two stages. First, they are trained, using a large dataset of text
  1860	from the Internet, to predict the next word. The models are then fine-tuned with additional data,
  1861	using an algorithm called reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), to produce outputs
  1862	that are preferred by human labelers.[10, 12, 13] Training language models on large text datasets
  1863	has given rise to capabilities such as few-shot learning[10] and the ability to carry out a wide range
  1864	of natural language tasks spanning different domains, including question answering, arithmetic, and
  1865	classification. Fine-tuning has made these models more controllable and useful.
  1866	1.1 Overview of findings and mitigations
  1867	In this system card,1 we outline the safety challenges that arise from GPT-4, and explain the
  1868	interventions we implemented to mitigate potential harms from its deployment. We focus on safety
  1869	challenges not because they necessarily outweigh the potential benefits,2 but because we wish to
  1870	motivate further work in safety measurement, mitigation, and assurance. The scope of this system
  1871	card is narrower than the potential scope of abilities GPT-4 can be used to unlock; notably, both
  1872	custom fine-tuning and image capabilities are explicitly out of scope.
  1873	We focus on analyzing two versions of the model: an early version fine-tuned for instruction
  1874	following (“GPT-4-early”); and a version fine-tuned for increased helpfulness and harmlessness[18]
  1875	that reflects the further mitigations outlined in this system card (“GPT-4-launch”).3 When we
  1876	discuss the risks of GPT-4 we will often refer to the behavior of GPT-4-early, because it reflects the
  1877	risks of GPT-4 when minimal safety mitigations are applied. In most cases, GPT-4-launch exhibits
  1878	much safer behavior due to the safety mitigations we applied.
  1879	Known risks associated with smaller language models are also present with GPT-4. GPT-4
  1880	can generate potentially harmful content, such as advice on planning attacks or hate speech. It
  1881	can represent various societal biases and worldviews that may not be representative of the users
  1882	intent,4 or of widely shared values. It can also generate code that is compromised or vulnerable.
  1883	The additional capabilities of GPT-4 also lead to new risk surfaces.
  1884	To understand the extent of these risks, we engaged more than 50 experts to help us gain a more
  1885	robust understanding of the GPT-4 model and potential deployment risks. We selected these areas
  1886	1This document takes inspiration from the concepts of model cards and system cards.[14, 15, 16] This document
  1887	often takes the system level of analysis, with that system including non-model mitigations such as use policies, access
  1888	controls, and monitoring for abuse
  1889	2See, e.g. discussion of Differential Technology Development in[17].
  1890	3We intentionally focus on these two versions instead of a comparison to the base GPT-4 model, since the base
  1891	model proved challenging for domain expert red teamers to use effectively to surface behaviors of interest.
  1892	4This includes tendencies to do things like repeat back a dialog user’s preferred answer (“sycophancy”), which can
  1893	worsen with scale.[19]

  1897	based on a number of factors, including prior observed risks in language models and AI systems,
  1898	and domains where we have observed increased user interest in the application of language models.
  1899	Workingwiththeseexpertsenabledustotestmodelbehaviorinhigh-riskareasthatrequireexpertise
  1900	to evaluate, as well as nascent risks that are poorly understood.
  1901	Through this analysis, we find that GPT-4 has the potential to be used to attempt to identify
  1902	private individuals when augmented with outside data. We also find that, although GPT-4’s
  1903	cybersecurity capabilities are not vastly superior to previous generations of LLMs, it does continue
  1904	the trend of potentially lowering the cost of certain steps of a successful cyberattack, such as through
  1905	social engineering or by enhancing existing security tools. Without safety mitigations, GPT-4 is
  1906	also able to give more detailed guidance on how to conduct harmful or illegal activities. Finally, we
  1907	facilitated a preliminary model evaluation by the Alignment Research Center (ARC) of GPT-4’s
  1908	ability to carry out actions to autonomously replicate5 and gather resources—a risk that, while
  1909	speculative, may become possible with sufficiently advanced AI systems—with the conclusion that
  1910	the current model is probably not yet capable of autonomously doing so.
  1911	Further research is needed to fully characterize these risks. In particular, we would like to see
  1912	work on more robust evaluations for the risk areas identified and more concrete measurements of the
  1913	prevalence of such behaviors across different language models, and to guide the development of these
  1914	models in safer directions. We are working on these types of evaluations, often in collaboration with
  1915	other research groups, with a focus on assessing risky emergent behaviors.
  1916	In addition to work on measurement, we aimed to mitigate the identified issues at various steps
  1917	of the development and deployment process. We reduced the prevalence of certain kinds of content
  1918	that violate our usage policies (such as inappropriate erotic content) in our pre-training dataset, and
  1919	fine-tuned the model to refuse certain instructions such as direct requests for illicit advice. We also
  1920	reduced the tendency of the models to hallucinate and, by leveraging data from prior model usage,
  1921	reduced the surface area of adversarial prompting or exploits (including attacks sometimes referred
  1922	to as “jailbreaks”) that the model succumbs to. Additionally, we trained a range of classifiers on
  1923	new risk vectors and have incorporated these into our monitoring workflow, enabling us to better
  1924	enforce our API usage policies. The effectiveness of these mitigations varies, but overall we were able
  1925	to significantly reduce the ease of producing various kinds of potentially harmful content, thereby
  1926	making GPT-4-launch significantly safer than GPT-4-early along these dimensions.
  1927	This system card is not comprehensive, and we expect to learn more over time about the
  1928	issues discussed below. Consistent with OpenAI’s deployment strategy,[21] we applied lessons from
  1929	earlier deployments and expect to apply lessons learned from this deployment both to make course
  1930	corrections and lay a foundation for future deployments.
  1931	Note that the examples included throughout this system card are not zero-shot and are cherry
  1932	picked from our evaluation efforts to illustrate specific types of safety concerns or harms. We included
  1933	examples to provide readers with context about the nature of the observed risks. One example is
  1934	not enough to show the breadth of ways these issues may manifest.
  1935	In Section 1, we outline some of the observed safety challenges in the development of GPT-4. In
  1936	Section 2, we discuss our process for deployment preparation and some of the model mitigations and
  1937	system safety measures. In Section 3, we conclude by discussing some remaining limitations and
  1938	recommendations in light of the observed risks we have learned through our iterative deployment
  1939	strategy.
  1940	5Autonomously replicate is a reference to self-replication, a concept that dates back at least as far as the 1988, to
  1941	the self-replicating computer worms, “Morris worm”, written by Robert Morris.[20]

  1945	2 GPT-4 Observed Safety Challenges
  1946	GPT-4 demonstrates increased performance in areas such as reasoning, knowledge retention, and
  1947	coding, compared to earlier models such as GPT-2[22] and GPT-3.[10] Many of these improvements
  1948	also present new safety challenges, which we highlight in this section.
  1949	We conducted a range of qualitative and quantitative evaluations of GPT-4. These evaluations
  1950	helped us gain an understanding of GPT-4’s capabilities, limitations, and risks; prioritize our
  1951	mitigation efforts; and iteratively test and build safer versions of the model. Some of the specific
  1952	risks we explored are:6
  1953	• Hallucinations
  1954	• Harmful content
  1955	• Harms of representation, allocation, and quality of service
  1956	• Disinformation and influence operations
  1957	• Proliferation of conventional and unconventional weapons
  1958	• Privacy
  1959	• Cybersecurity
  1960	• Potential for risky emergent behaviors
  1961	• Interactions with other systems
  1962	• Economic impacts
  1963	• Acceleration
  1964	• Overreliance
  1965	We found that GPT-4-early and GPT-4-launch exhibit many of the same limitations as earlier
  1966	language models, such as producing biased and unreliable content. Prior to our mitigations being
  1967	put in place, we also found that GPT-4-early presented increased risks in areas such as finding
  1968	websites selling illegal goods or services, and planning attacks. Additionally, the increased coherence
  1969	of the model enables it to generate content that may be more believable and more persuasive. We
  1970	elaborate on our evaluation procedure and findings below.
  1971	2.1 Evaluation Approach
  1972	2.1.1 Qualitative Evaluations
  1973	In August 2022, we began recruiting external experts to qualitatively probe, adversarially test, and
  1974	generally provide feedback on the GPT-4 models. This testing included stress testing, boundary

  1977	exclusive. Forexample,thingslikebias,misinformation,andharmfulcontentareoftendeeplyintertwinedanddrawing

  1979	consider in using them in, e.g., [24] and [25].

  1983	testing, and red teaming.7 We refer to these adversarial testing processes informally as “red teaming”
  1984	in line with the definition given in [27], namely“a structured effort to find flaws and vulnerabilities
  1985	in a plan, organization, or technical system, often performed by dedicated ’red teams’ that seek to
  1986	adopt an attacker’s mindset and methods.” We conducted internal adversarial testing GPT-4-launch
  1987	on March 10, 2023. We also tested multiple similar versions of GPT-4 in the lead-up to this
  1988	date, so analysis here is informed by that exploration as well. Red teaming has been applied to
  1989	language models in various ways: to reduce harmful outputs;[28] and to leverage external expertise
  1990	for domain-specific adversarial testing.[16] Some have explored red teaming language models using
  1991	language models.[29]
  1992	Red teaming in general, and the type of red teaming we call ’expert red teaming,’8 is just one of
  1993	the mechanisms[27] we use to inform our work identifying, measuring, and testing AI systems. Our
  1994	approach is to red team iteratively, starting with an initial hypothesis of which areas may be the
  1995	highest risk, testing these areas, and adjusting as we go. It is also iterative in the sense that we
  1996	use multiple rounds of red teaming as we incorporate new layers of mitigation and control, conduct
  1997	testing and refining, and repeat this process.
